preamble.summary: Proverbs 4 contains three distinct father-to-son speeches. The first (vv1-9) recalls the father's own education by his father, creating a three-generation chain of wisdom transmission: grandfather to father to son. The second (vv10-19) contrasts the path of the wise with the path of the wicked using the most vivid light-and-darkness imagery in the book. The third (vv20-27) turns inward, demanding that the son guard his heart above all else, because from it flow the springs of life.
preamble.remarkable: The three-generation chain of instruction in verses 1-9 is unique in Proverbs. The father says, 'When I was a son with my father, he taught me' — and then quotes his own father's words. The reader hears not one voice but two, layered together across time. Wisdom is not invented by each generation but received from the previous one and transmitted to the next. The path imagery in verses 10-19 reaches its apex in verse 18: 'The path of the righteous is like the light of dawn, growing brighter until full day.' This is one of the most beautiful sentences in the Hebrew Bible — and its force comes from the contrast with verse 19, where the way of the wicked is 'deep darkness' in which they cannot even see what makes them stumble. Verse 23 — 'Above all else, guard your heart, for from it flow the springs of life' — is the single most important anthropological statement in Proverbs. The heart (lev) is the command center of the entire person, and its protection is the non-negotiable priority.
preamble.friction: The idealized picture of wisdom transmission — father to son to grandson, smooth and unbroken — does not match the reality of most families, either in ancient Israel or today. Many of the Psalms and prophetic texts acknowledge that the chain breaks, that parents fail, and that children rebel. Proverbs 4 presents the ideal without acknowledging the fractures, which is characteristic of wisdom literature's tendency toward clean patterns. The command to guard the heart (v23) also raises the question of how one guards something that is, according to Jeremiah 17:9, 'deceitful above all things and beyond cure.' The tension between Proverbs' confidence in human moral agency and Jeremiah's suspicion of the human heart is never fully resolved within the Old Testament.
preamble.connections: The three-generation instruction chain connects to Deuteronomy 6:7 ('teach them diligently to your children') and Psalm 78:1-8, which describes the deliberate transmission of God's acts across generations. The light-of-dawn image (v18) is echoed in Isaiah 58:8 ('your light will break forth like the dawn') and 2 Samuel 23:4 (David's last words about a righteous ruler). The heart-guarding command (v23) anticipates Jesus' teaching that what comes out of the heart defiles a person (Mark 7:20-23) and Paul's instruction to guard what has been entrusted (2 Timothy 1:14).

verses.text_hebrew: שִׁמְע֣וּ בָ֭נִים מ֣וּסַר אָ֑ב וְ֝הַקְשִׁ֗יבוּ לָדַ֥עַת בִּינָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Hear, ye children, the instruction of a father, and attend to know understanding.
verses.rendering:
Listen, sons, to a father's discipline,
and pay attention, so you may gain understanding.
translator_notes: The plural banim ('sons, children') expands the audience beyond the single 'my son' of previous chapters. Musar av ('discipline of a father') is presented as a category of knowledge worth having. The verb haqshivu ('pay attention, listen carefully') from qashav demands focused hearing, not mere auditory reception.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י לֶ֣קַח ט֭וֹב נָתַ֣תִּי לָכֶ֑ם תּֽ֝וֹרָתִ֗י אַֽל־תַּעֲזֹֽבוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: For I give you good doctrine, forsake ye not my law.
verses.rendering:
For I give you sound teaching;
do not abandon my instruction.
translator_notes: Leqach tov ('good teaching, sound learning') and torati ('my instruction') are offered as reliable, tested goods. The verb ta'azovu ('abandon, forsake') warns against the apostasy that begins not with dramatic rebellion but with gradual neglect.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי־בֵ֣ן הָ֭יִיתִי לְאָבִ֑י רַ֥ךְ וְ֝יָחִ֗יד לִפְנֵ֥י אִמִּֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: For I was my father's son, tender and only beloved in the sight of my mother.
verses.rendering:
For I was a son to my father,
tender and precious in my mother's sight.
translator_notes: The father reveals his own backstory: he too was rakh ('tender, soft, young, vulnerable') and yachid ('only one, beloved, precious') before his mother. The shift to autobiography creates intimacy — the father is not a distant authority but someone who once sat where the son sits now, receiving instruction from his own parents.

verses.text_hebrew: וַיֹּרֵ֗נִי וַיֹּ֣אמֶר לִ֭י יִתְמָךְ־דְּבָרַ֣י לִבֶּ֑ךָ שְׁמֹ֖ר מִצְוֺתַ֣י וֶחְיֵֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: He taught me also, and said unto me, Let thine heart retain my words: keep my commandments, and live.
verses.rendering:
He taught me and said to me,
'Let your heart hold fast to my words.
Keep my commands and live.
translator_notes: Now the grandfather speaks through the father's memory. The imperative vechyeh ('and live!') is stark — obedience to wisdom is not optional enrichment but the condition of survival. The verb yitmakh ('hold fast, support, sustain') describes the heart gripping the father's words like a hand gripping a lifeline.

verses.text_hebrew: קְנֵ֣ה חָ֭כְמָה קְנֵ֣ה בִינָ֑ה אַל־תִּשְׁכַּ֥ח וְאַל־תֵּ֝֗ט מֵאִמְרֵי־פִֽי׃
verses.text_kjv: Get wisdom, get understanding: forget it not; neither decline from the words of my mouth.
verses.rendering:
Acquire wisdom, acquire understanding!
Do not forget, and do not turn from the words of my mouth.
translator_notes: The doubled imperative qeneh ('acquire, buy, purchase') treats wisdom and binah ('understanding') as commodities to be pursued and bought at any price. The verb qanah implies exchange — you must give something up to get wisdom. The grandfather's voice continues with urgency: forget nothing, deviate from nothing.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תַּעַזְבֶ֥הָ וְתִנְצְרֶ֑ךָּ אֱהָבֶ֥הָ וְתִשְׁמְרֶֽךָּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Forsake her not, and she shall preserve thee: love her, and she shall keep thee.
verses.rendering:
Do not abandon her, and she will protect you;
love her, and she will guard you.
translator_notes: Wisdom (feminine in Hebrew) is again personified as a woman deserving loyalty. The reciprocal structure is important: do not abandon her — she will protect you; love her — she will guard you. The relationship between the wise person and wisdom mirrors a covenant bond: faithfulness produces protection.

verses.text_hebrew: רֵאשִׁ֣ית חָ֭כְמָה קְנֵ֣ה חָכְמָ֑ה וּבְכָל־קִ֝נְיָנְךָ֗ קְנֵ֣ה בִינָֽה׃
verses.text_kjv: Wisdom is the principal thing; therefore get wisdom: and with all thy getting get understanding.
verses.rendering:
The beginning of wisdom: acquire wisdom!
And with all you acquire, acquire understanding.
verses.expanded_rendering: Reshit chokmah ('the beginning of wisdom, the principal thing about wisdom') is to qeneh chokmah ('acquire wisdom') — a deliberately circular statement. The starting point of wisdom is the decision to pursue wisdom. Before you can be wise, you must want to be wise. The second line raises the stakes: uvekhol qinyanekha ('and with all your acquisitions, with everything you acquire') qeneh vinah ('acquire understanding'). The verb qanah appears four times in verses 5-7, hammering the point with repetition. Whatever else you buy, purchase, earn, or accumulate — make sure understanding is part of the transaction. The grandfather is saying: everything you possess is worthless if understanding is not among your possessions.
translator_notes: The apparent circularity of 'the beginning of wisdom is: acquire wisdom' is not tautology but insistence. Wisdom begins with the resolve to seek it. Without that initial commitment, no amount of intelligence or opportunity will produce wisdom. The choice precedes the achievement.

verses.text_hebrew: סַלְסְלֶ֥הָ וּֽתְרוֹמְמֶ֑ךָּ תְּ֝כַבֵּ֗דְךָ כִּ֣י תְחַבְּקֶֽנָּה׃
verses.text_kjv: Exalt her, and she shall promote thee: she shall bring thee to honour, when thou dost embrace her.
verses.rendering:
Cherish her, and she will lift you up;
she will honor you when you embrace her.
translator_notes: The verb salselehah ('cherish, exalt, esteem highly') is rare and may derive from salal ('to heap up, to lift'). The imagery of embracing (techabbeqennah) wisdom continues the marital metaphor — wisdom is a partner to be held close, and she reciprocates with honor (tekhabbedkha, 'she will honor you, give you weight').

verses.text_hebrew: תִּתֵּ֣ן לְ֭רֹאשְׁךָ לִוְיַת־חֵ֑ן עֲטֶ֖רֶת תִּפְאֶ֣רֶת תְּמַגְּנֶֽךָּ׃
verses.text_kjv: She shall give to thine head an ornament of grace: a crown of glory shall she deliver to thee.
verses.rendering:
She will set a garland of grace on your head;
a crown of splendor she will bestow on you.'
translator_notes: The grandfather's quoted speech ends with the image of wisdom crowning the faithful student. The livyat chen ('garland of grace') from 1:9 returns, now joined by ateret tif'eret ('crown of splendor, glorious crown'). Wisdom's reward is public honor — the crown is visible to the community.

verses.text_hebrew: שְׁמַ֣ע בְּ֭נִי וְקַ֣ח אֲמָרָ֑י וְיִרְבּ֥וּ לְ֝ךָ֗ שְׁנ֣וֹת חַיִּֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Hear, O my son, and receive my sayings; and the years of thy life shall be many.
verses.rendering:
Listen, my son, and receive my words,
and the years of your life will be many.
translator_notes: The second speech begins with a return to the singular 'my son.' The promise of shenot chayyim ('years of life') connects back to 3:2 — long life is the consistent reward of heeding wisdom.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּדֶ֣רֶךְ חָ֭כְמָה הֹרֵתִ֑יךָ הִ֝דְרַכְתִּ֗יךָ בְּמַעְגְּלֵי־יֹֽשֶׁר׃
verses.text_kjv: I have taught thee in the way of wisdom; I have led thee in right paths.
verses.rendering:
I have directed you in the way of wisdom;
I have guided you along straight paths.
translator_notes: The father claims to have horetikha ('directed you, instructed you') — from yarah, the same root as torah. He has also hidrakhttikha ('led you, caused you to walk') in ma'gele yosher ('tracks of uprightness'). The ma'gal ('track, rut') is a worn path — the father has placed the son on well-established routes, not experimental ones.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּֽ֭לֶכְתְּךָ לֹא־יֵצַ֣ר צַעֲדֶ֑ךָ וְאִם־תָּ֝ר֗וּץ לֹ֣א תִכָּשֵֽׁל׃
verses.text_kjv: When thou goest, thy steps shall not be straitened; and when thou runnest, thou shalt not stumble.
verses.rendering:
When you walk, your stride will not be hindered;
and when you run, you will not stumble.
translator_notes: Two speeds of travel — walking and running — are both covered by wisdom's protection. The verb yetsar ('be constricted, narrowed, hindered') describes a path too tight for normal movement. Wisdom clears the way so that even at full speed (taruts, 'you run'), there is no stumbling (tikhashel).

verses.text_hebrew: הַחֲזֵ֣ק בַּמּוּסָ֣ר אַל־תֶּ֑רֶף נִ֝צְּרֶ֗הָ כִּי־הִ֥יא חַיֶּֽיךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Take fast hold of instruction; let her not go: keep her; for she is thy life.
verses.rendering:
Hold fast to discipline — do not let go!
Guard her, for she is your life.
translator_notes: The imperative hachazeg ('hold fast, grip tightly') combined with al teref ('do not let go, do not relax your grip') conveys desperate urgency. The reason is absolute: ki hi chayyekha ('for she is your life'). Discipline is not supplementary to life; it is life itself. Without it, the son dies.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּאֹ֣רַח רְ֭שָׁעִים אַל־תָּבֹ֑א וְאַל־תְּ֝אַשֵּׁ֗ר בְּדֶ֣רֶךְ רָעִֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Enter not into the path of the wicked, and go not in the way of evil men.
verses.rendering:
Do not enter the path of the wicked,
and do not walk in the way of evildoers.
translator_notes: The negative commands mirror the positive ones: just as the son was guided onto straight paths (v11), he must now refuse the orach resha'im ('path of the wicked') and derekh ra'im ('way of evil men'). The verb te'asher ('step forward, walk, proceed') is from the same root as ashre ('fortunate') — there is no fortune on this road.

verses.text_hebrew: פְּרָעֵ֥הוּ אַל־תַּעֲבָר־בּ֑וֹ שְׂטֵ֥ה מֵ֝עָלָ֗יו וַעֲבֽוֹר׃
verses.text_kjv: Avoid it, pass not by it, turn from it, and pass away.
verses.rendering:
Avoid it — do not travel on it;
turn away from it and pass on.
translator_notes: Four imperatives in rapid succession: pera'ehu ('avoid it, let it alone'), al ta'avor bo ('do not pass through it'), seteh me'alav ('turn aside from it'), and avor ('pass on, go your way'). The staccato commands convey urgency — do not linger near this road, do not even slow down to look at it.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֤י לֹ֣א יִ֭שְׁנוּ אִם־לֹ֣א יָרֵ֑עוּ וְנִגְזְלָ֥ה שְׁ֝נָתָ֗ם אִם־לֹ֥א יַכְשִֽׁילוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: For they sleep not, except they have done mischief; and their sleep is taken away, unless they cause some to fall.
verses.rendering:
For they cannot sleep unless they have done harm;
their sleep is stolen unless they have made someone stumble.
translator_notes: The wicked suffer from an inverted insomnia: they cannot rest until they have done evil. Their shnatam ('their sleep') is nigzelah ('robbed, stolen') unless they have caused someone to yakhshilu ('stumble, fall'). Evil has become their sedative. The verse diagnoses a condition — addiction to malice — that makes the wicked perpetual threats to others.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּ֣י לָ֭חֲמוּ לֶ֣חֶם רֶ֑שַׁע וְיֵ֖ין חֲמָסִ֣ים יִשְׁתּֽוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: For they eat the bread of wickedness, and drink the wine of violence.
verses.rendering:
For wickedness is the bread they eat
and drink the wine of violence.
translator_notes: Wickedness and violence have become their daily sustenance — lechem resha ('bread of wickedness') and yen chamasim ('wine of violence'). The food metaphor implies that evil nourishes them the way bread and wine nourish the body. They feed on harm. The imagery inverts the normal provision of God, who gives bread and wine as blessings.

verses.text_hebrew: וְאֹ֣רַח צַ֭דִּיקִים כְּא֣וֹר נֹ֑גַהּ הוֹלֵ֥ךְ וָ֝א֗וֹר עַד־נְכ֥וֹן הַיּֽוֹם׃
verses.text_kjv: But the path of the just is as the shining light, that shineth more and more unto the perfect day.
verses.rendering:
But the path of the righteous is like the light of dawn,
shining brighter and brighter until full day.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the chapter's crown jewel. The orach tsaddiqim ('path of the righteous') is compared to or nogah ('the light of brightness, the dawn light'). The participle holekh va-or ('going and shining, growing brighter') captures a gradual, inexorable increase — the righteous life does not achieve instant clarity but grows in illumination the way morning light intensifies from first gray to full noon. The phrase ad nekhon ha-yom ('until the established day, until the day is firm, until full day') describes the moment when the sun stands at its zenith and every shadow is eliminated. The metaphor promises progressive revelation: the wise do not see everything at once, but what they see becomes clearer and clearer. The path itself generates light. Walking it produces vision. This is the opposite of the wicked, whose path is darkness (v19) — they stumble because they cannot see what they are tripping over. The righteous see more because they walk more; they walk more because they see more. Light and movement reinforce each other.
translator_notes: The phrase 'light of dawn' rather than 'shining light' preserves the temporal metaphor — this is not a spotlight but a sunrise, a process that unfolds over time. The righteous life is not static but dynamic, not a fixed state but a trajectory.

verses.text_hebrew: דֶּ֣רֶךְ רְ֭שָׁעִים כָּאֲפֵלָ֑ה לֹ֥א יָ֝דְע֗וּ בַּמֶּ֥ה יִכָּשֵֽׁלוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: The way of the wicked is as darkness: they know not at what they stumble.
verses.rendering:
The way of the wicked is like deep darkness;
they do not know what makes them stumble.
translator_notes: The contrast is devastating. Against the crescendo of dawn light stands afelah ('deep darkness, thick gloom') — not ordinary night but impenetrable blackness. The final clause is the most damning: lo yade'u bammeh yikkashelu ('they do not know what they stumble over'). The wicked do not merely fall; they fall without understanding why. Their ignorance of their own destruction is total. The darkness is not just around them but inside them.

verses.text_hebrew: בְּ֭נִי לִדְבָרַ֣י הַקְשִׁ֑יבָה לַ֝אֲמָרַ֗י הַט־אָזְנֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: My son, attend to my words; incline thine ear unto my sayings.
verses.rendering:
My son, pay attention to my words;
incline your ear to my sayings.
translator_notes: The third speech begins with familiar imperative language: haqshivah ('pay attention') and hat oznekha ('incline your ear'). The body must cooperate — ears must be physically turned toward the speaker.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־יַלִּ֥יזוּ מֵעֵינֶ֑יךָ שָׁ֝מְרֵ֗ם בְּת֣וֹךְ לְבָבֶֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Let them not depart from thine eyes; keep them in the midst of thine heart.
verses.rendering:
Do not let them slip from your eyes;
keep them deep within your heart.
translator_notes: The words must not yallizu ('slip away, depart') from the eyes and must be kept betokh levavekha ('in the midst of your heart, at the center of your inner life'). The dual retention — visual and internal — means both constant attention and deep internalization.

verses.text_hebrew: כִּי־חַיִּ֣ים הֵ֭ם לְמֹצְאֵיהֶ֑ם וּֽלְכָל־בְּשָׂר֥וֹ מַרְפֵּֽא׃
verses.text_kjv: For they are life unto those that find them, and health to all their flesh.
verses.rendering:
For they are life to those who find them
and healing for the whole body.
translator_notes: The father's words are chayyim ('life') and marpe ('healing, remedy, cure') for all flesh. The claim is holistic: wisdom heals the entire person, body included. The connection between right living and physical health runs throughout Proverbs, reflecting the Hebrew integration of moral and physical well-being.

verses.text_hebrew: מִֽכָּל־מִ֭שְׁמָר נְצֹ֣ר לִבֶּ֑ךָ כִּי־מִ֝מֶּ֗נּוּ תּוֹצְא֥וֹת חַיִּֽים׃
verses.text_kjv: Keep thy heart with all diligence; for out of it are the issues of life.
verses.rendering:
Above all that you guard, guard your heart,
for from it flow the springs of life.
verses.expanded_rendering: This is the anthropological center of the entire book. Mikkol mishmar ('above every guarded thing, more than anything you guard') netsor libbeka ('guard your heart'). The mishmar ('guarded thing, place of guarding, prison, watch') implies that the son is already guarding many things — possessions, reputation, relationships — but the heart must be guarded above them all. The reason is ki mimmennu totse'ot chayyim ('for from it are the outgoings of life, the springs of life'). The word totse'ot ('outgoings, issues, exits, springs') from yatsa ('to go out') describes the heart as a source from which life flows outward. Everything the person does, says, thinks, and becomes originates in the lev. If the heart is corrupted, every downstream expression will be corrupted. If the heart is guarded, everything that flows from it will carry that protection. The heart is not one organ among many; it is the origin point. Guard it, and you guard everything. Lose it, and you lose everything.
translator_notes: We render mikkol mishmar as 'above all that you guard' to preserve the comparative force of the Hebrew — the son is not told to guard his heart instead of other things but more than other things. The heart is the highest-priority asset in the wise person's security system.

The lev in Hebrew encompasses intellect, will, emotion, and moral judgment — it is the entire inner person. English 'heart' tends toward emotion only, which is too narrow. The Hebrew command is to guard the command center, not just the feelings.

key_terms.semantic_range: heart, mind, will, inner self, understanding, intention, courage; the command center of the entire person
key_terms.note: The lev in Hebrew thought is not primarily the seat of emotion (as in English) but the seat of intellect, will, and moral decision-making. When Proverbs says 'guard your heart,' it means guard your thinking, your choosing, your intending — the whole interior architecture from which all action proceeds.

verses.text_hebrew: הָסֵ֣ר מִ֭מְּךָ עִקְּשׁ֣וּת פֶּ֑ה וּלְז֥וּת שְׂ֝פָתַ֗יִם הַרְחֵ֥ק מִמֶּֽךָּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Put away from thee a froward mouth, and perverse lips put far from thee.
verses.rendering:
Put away from you crooked speech,
and keep devious talk far from you.
translator_notes: From the heart (v23) to the mouth (v24): iqqeshut peh ('crookedness of mouth') and lezut sefatayim ('deviousness of lips') are the first expressions of a corrupted heart. The progression from heart to speech to eyes to feet (vv23-27) traces the outward flow of life from its source.

verses.text_hebrew: עֵ֭ינֶיךָ לְנֹ֣כַח יַבִּ֑יטוּ וְ֝עַפְעַפֶּ֗יךָ יַיְשִׁ֥רוּ נֶגְדֶּֽךָ׃
verses.text_kjv: Let thine eyes look right on, and let thine eyelids look straight before thee.
verses.rendering:
Let your eyes look straight ahead,
and your gaze be fixed directly before you.
translator_notes: From speech to sight: the eyes (eynekha) must look lenokhach ('straight ahead, to what is in front') and the eyelids (af'appekha, used poetically for the gaze) must yayshiru ('be straight, be directed') negdekha ('before you, in front of you'). The command is for focused, undistracted vision — no sideways glances at the paths of the wicked.

verses.text_hebrew: פַּ֭לֵּס מַעְגַּ֣ל רַגְלֶ֑ךָ וְֽכָל־דְּרָכֶ֥יךָ יִכֹּֽנוּ׃
verses.text_kjv: Ponder the path of thy feet, and let all thy ways be established.
verses.rendering:
Make level the path for your feet,
and all your ways will be sure.
translator_notes: The verb palles ('make level, weigh, ponder, prepare') has construction overtones — prepare the road before you walk it. The ma'gal raglekha ('track of your feet') must be leveled in advance. The result: kol derakhekha yikkonu ('all your ways will be established, firm, sure'). Deliberate preparation produces stable paths.

verses.text_hebrew: אַל־תֵּט־יָמִ֥ין וּשְׂמֹ֑אול הָסֵ֖ר רַגְלְךָ֣ מֵרָֽע׃
verses.text_kjv: Turn not to the right hand nor to the left: remove thy foot from evil.
verses.rendering:
Do not turn to the right or to the left;
remove your foot from evil.
translator_notes: The chapter ends with the Deuteronomic formula: al tet yamin usmol ('do not turn right or left') — the identical instruction given to Israel regarding the Law (Deuteronomy 5:32, 17:11, 28:14) and to Joshua (Joshua 1:7). The son is to walk wisdom's path with the same unwavering directness demanded of Israel at Sinai. The final imperative — haser raglekha mera ('remove your foot from evil') — is physical: take your foot off that path. Now.
